title: Kaitiaki o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku | Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our college lands and holds deep significance for Ngāti Kahungunu. According to local pūrākau, the stream was created by the tears of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden, as she mourned the loss of her mortal lover. Students will learn how our tūpuna used scientific observation of tuna (eels), kōura (freshwater crayfish), and aquatic plants to monitor the awa's mauri (life force), connecting traditional knowledge with modern water quality testing methods.
learningObjectives: Conduct and analyze at least three scientific water quality tests (pH, turbidity, nitrate levels) at two different sites along Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Compare traditional Māori environmental indicators (presence of tuna, kōura, specific riparian plants) with modern scientific water quality data to draw conclusions about stream health
Design and implement a practical kaitiakitanga action (e.g., planting native riparian species, creating educational signage) based on scientific findings to improve the mauri of the awa
Present scientific data and kaitiakitanga recommendations using both Western scientific conventions and te reo Māori environmental terminology

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa | Connecting to the Stream
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua who shares the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi and demonstrates traditional methods of observing stream health through tuna behavior, kōura presence, and aquatic plants. Students sketch the stream ecosystem and record initial observations.

activities.title: Pūtaiao Mea Ine Wai | Scientific Water Testing
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students collect and analyze water samples from two sites along the awa (upstream near the source and downstream near college grounds). They measure pH, turbidity, nitrate levels, and temperature, recording data in both digital and field journal formats.

activities.title: Hono Pūtaiao Māori ki Pūtaiao Pākehā | Integrating Knowledge Systems
activities.description: In small groups, students create comparative tables that align traditional indicators (e.g., 'clear water with many kōura' = good health) with scientific measurements (e.g., 'pH 6.5-7.5, nitrates < 1mg/L'). They identify correlations and discrepancies between knowledge systems.

activities.title: Mahi Kaitiaki | Guardianship in Action
activities.description: Based on their findings, students design and implement a practical kaitiakitanga project. Options include: planting harakeke and other native riparian species to reduce bank erosion, creating bilingual educational signs about stream health, or building and placing tuna hāpua (eel resting habitats) in the stream.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga Pūtaiao | Science Presentation
activities.description: Groups create multimedia presentations that share their scientific data, analysis, and kaitiakitanga actions. Presentations must include both scientific terminology (graphs, data tables) and te reo Māori concepts (mauri, kaitiakitanga, whakapapa of the awa), presented to community members and local iwi representatives.

resources: Hanna Instruments HI98129 pH/EC/TDS/Temp Meter for water quality testing
Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hōri Waititi (expert in traditional ecological knowledge of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku)
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources and guidelines
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Te Awa Māori - Māori Rivers and Streams' for cultural context

tasks: Water quality testing field journal with completed data tables and analysis questions
Comparative chart aligning traditional Māori indicators with scientific measurements
Group multimedia presentation showcasing findings and kaitiakitanga action
Reflective paragraph on how integrating mātauranga Māori and Western science deepened understanding of kaitiakitanga

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in Science - Students will carry out science investigations using a variety of approaches
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth Systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic interactions with other Earth systems
Science in the New Zealand Curriculum: Vision - Students will develop the knowledge, skills, and attitudes to enable them to participate as critical, informed, and responsible citizens
